* [Thēthi](/wiki/Th%C4%93thi "Thēthi")-Maithili * [Eastern Maithili](/wiki/Eastern_Maithili "Eastern Maithili") * [Angika](/wiki/Angika "Angika")-Maithili (also known as Southern Standard Maithili) * [Bajjika](/wiki/Bajjika "Bajjika")-Maithili * Sotipura-Maithili * Possibly [Kochila Tharu](/wiki/Kochila_Tharu "Kochila Tharu")[[3]](#cite_note-Driem2022-3)

* [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") (Official) * [Tirhuta script](/wiki/Tirhuta_script "Tirhuta script") (Historical and Original Script of Maithili language) * [Newar script](/wiki/Newar_script "Newar script")[[4]](#cite_note-4) (Historical) * [Kaithi script](/wiki/Kaithi_script "Kaithi script")[[5]](#cite_note-5) (Historical)

* [India](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India "Languages with official status in India")   + [Jharkhand](/wiki/Jharkhand "Jharkhand")[[6]](#cite_note-6) (additional) * [Nepal](/wiki/Languages_of_Nepal "Languages of Nepal")   + [Koshi Province](/wiki/Koshi_Province "Koshi Province")   + [Madhesh Province](/wiki/Madhesh_Province "Madhesh Province")

[Sahitya Akademi](/wiki/Sahitya_Akademi "Sahitya Akademi"), [Maithili Academy](/wiki/Maithili_Academy "Maithili Academy"), [Maithili - Bhojpuri Academy, Delhi](/wiki/Maithili_-_Bhojpuri_Academy,_Delhi "Maithili - Bhojpuri Academy, Delhi"), [Nepal Academy](/wiki/Nepal_Academy "Nepal Academy")

([Tirhuta:](/wiki/Tirhuta_script "Tirhuta script") 𑒧𑒻𑒟𑒱𑒪𑒲, [/ˈmaɪtɪli/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*MY-til-ee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) Maithili: [[ˈməi̯tʰɪliː]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA")) is an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_language "Indo-Aryan language") spoken in parts of [India](/wiki/India "India") and [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"). It is native to the [Mithila region](/wiki/Mithila_(region) "Mithila (region)"), which encompasses parts of the eastern Indian states of [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") and [Jharkhand](/wiki/Jharkhand "Jharkhand") as well as Nepal's [Koshi](/wiki/Koshi_Province "Koshi Province") and [Madhesh Provinces](/wiki/Madhesh_Province "Madhesh Province"). It is one of the 22 scheduled [languages of India](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India").[[8]](#cite_note-mha.nic.in-8)[[9]](#cite_note-script_experts_meet-9)[[10]](#cite_note-m.livehindustan.com-10) It is the second most commonly spoken native [Nepalese language](/wiki/Languages_of_Nepal "Languages of Nepal") constitutionally[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENational_Statistics_Office202332-11)[[12]](#cite_note-e27-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Sah2013-13) registered as one of the fourteen provincial official [languages of Nepal](/wiki/Languages_of_Nepal "Languages of Nepal").[[14]](#cite_note-langcomreport-14)

**Maithili** ([Tirhuta:](/wiki/Tirhuta_script "Tirhuta script") 𑒧𑒻𑒟𑒱𑒪𑒲, [/ˈmaɪtɪli/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*MY-til-ee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) Maithili: [[ˈməi̯tʰɪliː]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA")) is an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_language "Indo-Aryan language") spoken in parts of [India](/wiki/India "India") and [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"). It is native to the [Mithila region](/wiki/Mithila_(region) "Mithila (region)"), which encompasses parts of the eastern Indian states of [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") and [Jharkhand](/wiki/Jharkhand "Jharkhand") as well as Nepal's [Koshi](/wiki/Koshi_Province "Koshi Province") and [Madhesh Provinces](/wiki/Madhesh_Province "Madhesh Province"). It is one of the 22 scheduled [languages of India](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India").[[8]](#cite_note-mha.nic.in-8)[[9]](#cite_note-script_experts_meet-9)[[10]](#cite_note-m.livehindustan.com-10) It is the second most commonly spoken native [Nepalese language](/wiki/Languages_of_Nepal "Languages of Nepal") constitutionally[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENational_Statistics_Office202332-11)[[12]](#cite_note-e27-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Sah2013-13) registered as one of the fourteen provincial official [languages of Nepal](/wiki/Languages_of_Nepal "Languages of Nepal").[[14]](#cite_note-langcomreport-14)

It is spoken by 17 million people. Of those, 3.2 million are [Nepalese speakers](/wiki/Nepalis "Nepalis").[[12]](#cite_note-e27-12) The language is predominantly written in [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari"), but the historical [Tirhuta](/wiki/Tirhuta_script "Tirhuta script") and [Kaithi](/wiki/Kaithi "Kaithi") scripts retain some use until today.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Official status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maithili_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Official status")]

In 2003, Maithili was included in the [Eighth Schedule](/wiki/8th_Schedule "8th Schedule") of the [Indian Constitution](/wiki/Indian_Constitution "Indian Constitution") as a recognised [Indian language](/wiki/Language_of_India "Language of India"), which allows it to be used in education, government, and other official contexts in India.[[8]](#cite_note-mha.nic.in-8) In March 2018, Maithili received the second official language status in the [Indian state](/wiki/Indian_state "Indian state") of [Jharkhand](/wiki/Jharkhand "Jharkhand").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[Gopal Jee Thakur](/wiki/Gopal_Jee_Thakur "Gopal Jee Thakur") of the [Bharatiya Janata Party](/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_Party "Bharatiya Janata Party") is the first [Member of Parliament, Lok Sabha](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament,_Lok_Sabha "Member of Parliament, Lok Sabha") who speaks in the Maithili language in the [Parliament of India](/wiki/Parliament_of_India "Parliament of India").[[17]](#cite_note-17) He is currently the MP for [Darbhanga](/wiki/Darbhanga_Lok_Sabha_constituency "Darbhanga Lok Sabha constituency").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The [Nepalese Languages Commission](/wiki/Language_Commission_(Nepal) "Language Commission (Nepal)") has made Maithili an official Nepalese language used for administration in [Koshi province](/wiki/Koshi_province "Koshi province") and [Madhesh Province](/wiki/Madhesh_Province "Madhesh Province").[[14]](#cite_note-langcomreport-14)

On 26 November 2024, during the occasion of the [Constitution Day](/wiki/Constitution_Day_(India) "Constitution Day (India)"), [Maithili version of the Indian constitution](/wiki/Maithili_version_of_Indian_Constitution "Maithili version of Indian Constitution") was launched by the [President of India](/wiki/President_of_India "President of India") [Droupadi Murmu](/wiki/Droupadi_Murmu "Droupadi Murmu").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maithili_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Distribution")]

In India, it is mainly spoken in [Darbhanga](/wiki/Darbhanga_division "Darbhanga division"), [Madhubani](/wiki/Madhubani,_India "Madhubani, India"), [Tirhut](/wiki/Tirhut_division "Tirhut division"), [Kosi](/wiki/Kosi_division "Kosi division"), [Purnia](/wiki/Purnia_division "Purnia division"), [Bhagalpur](/wiki/Bhagalpur_division "Bhagalpur division") and [Munger](/wiki/Munger_division "Munger division") divisions of [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") and in [Santhal Pargana](/wiki/Santhal_Pargana_division "Santhal Pargana division") division of [Jharkhand](/wiki/Jharkhand "Jharkhand").[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In Nepal, Maithili is spoken in [Madhesh Province](/wiki/Madhesh_Province "Madhesh Province") and [Koshi Province](/wiki/Koshi_Province "Koshi Province").[[12]](#cite_note-e27-12)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENational_Statistics_Office2023210,_212,_218–220-22)

[Darbhanga](/wiki/Darbhanga "Darbhanga"), [Madhubani](/wiki/Madhubani,_India "Madhubani, India"), and [Janakpur](/wiki/Janakpur "Janakpur") constitutes important cultural and linguistic centers of Maithili language.[[12]](#cite_note-e27-12)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maithili_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Classification")]

In 1870s, [Beames](/wiki/John_Beames "John Beames") considered Maithili a dialect of a [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"). [Hoernlé](/wiki/Rudolf_Hoernl%C3%A9 "Rudolf Hoernlé") initially treated it as a dialect of [Eastern Hindi](/wiki/Eastern_Hindi "Eastern Hindi"), but after comparing it with the [Gaudian](/wiki/Gau%E1%B8%8Da_(region) "Gauḍa (region)") languages, recognised that it shows more similarities with the [Bengali language](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") than with [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi"). [Grierson](/wiki/George_Abraham_Grierson "George Abraham Grierson") recognised it as a distinct language, grouped under '[Bihari](/wiki/Bihari_languages "Bihari languages")' and published its first [grammar](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar") in 1881.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Yadav1979-24)

[Chatterji](/wiki/Suniti_Kumar_Chatterji "Suniti Kumar Chatterji") grouped Maithili with the [Magadhi Prakrit](/wiki/Magadhi_Prakrit "Magadhi Prakrit").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maithili_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Dialects")]

Maithili varies greatly in dialects.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

* The standard form of Maithili is Sotipura also called Central Maithili[[27]](#cite_note-27) which is mainly spoken in [Darbhanga](/wiki/Darbhanga_district "Darbhanga district"), [Madhubani](/wiki/Madhubani_District "Madhubani District"), [Supaul](/wiki/Supaul_District "Supaul District"), [Madhepura](/wiki/Madhepura_district "Madhepura district"), [Purnia](/wiki/Purnia_district "Purnia district"), [Samastipur](/wiki/Samastipur_district "Samastipur district"), [Araria](/wiki/Araria_district "Araria district") and [Saharsa](/wiki/Saharsa_district "Saharsa district") districts of [India](/wiki/India "India") and in [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") it is spoken in [Dhanusha](/wiki/Dhanusha_District "Dhanusha District"), [Mahottari](/wiki/Mahottari_District "Mahottari District"), [Siraha](/wiki/Siraha_District "Siraha District"), [Saptari](/wiki/Saptari_District "Saptari District"), [Sarlahi](/wiki/Sarlahi_District "Sarlahi District") and [Sunsari](/wiki/Sunsari_District "Sunsari District"), and [Morang](/wiki/Morang_District "Morang District") districts.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* [Bajjika](/wiki/Bajjika "Bajjika") dialect also known as Western Maithili [[29]](#cite_note-29) is mainly spoken in [Sitamarhi](/wiki/Sitamarhi_district "Sitamarhi district"), [Muzaffarpur](/wiki/Muzaffarpur_district "Muzaffarpur district"), [Vaishali](/wiki/Vaishali_district "Vaishali district") and [Sheohar](/wiki/Sheohar_district "Sheohar district") districts of [Bihar, India](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") & [Rautahat](/wiki/Rautahat_District "Rautahat District"), and [Sarlahi](/wiki/Sarlahi_District "Sarlahi District") districts of [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal").[[30]](#cite_note-30) It is listed as a distinct language in Nepal and overlaps by 76–86% with Maithili dialects spoken in Dhanusa, [Morang](/wiki/Morang_District "Morang District"), Saptari and Sarlahi Districts.[[31]](#cite_note-ethnologue2-31)
* [Thēthi](/wiki/Th%C4%93thi "Thēthi") dialect is spoken mainly in [Kosi](/wiki/Kosi_division "Kosi division"), [Purnia](/wiki/Purnia_division "Purnia division") and [Munger divisions](/wiki/Munger_division "Munger division") and [Mokama](/wiki/Mokama "Mokama") in Bihar and some adjoining districts of Nepal.[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* [Angika](/wiki/Angika "Angika") dialect is mainly spoken in and around [Bhagalpur](/wiki/Bhagalpur "Bhagalpur"), [Banka](/wiki/Banka,_Bihar "Banka, Bihar"),[[33]](#cite_note-Language-33) [Munger](/wiki/Munger "Munger")[[34]](#cite_note-34) districts of [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") and [Godda](/wiki/Godda_district "Godda district"), [Sahebganj](/wiki/Sahebganj_district "Sahebganj district"), [Dumka](/wiki/Dumka_district "Dumka district"), districts of [Jharkhand](/wiki/Jharkhand "Jharkhand").[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* [Kochila Tharu](/wiki/Kochila_Tharu "Kochila Tharu"), a language spoken by the [Tharu people](/wiki/Tharu_people "Tharu people") in the districts of [Saptari](/wiki/Saptari_district "Saptari district") and [Siraha](/wiki/Siraha_district "Siraha district") as well as the adjoining areas. Some linguists have described it as a variety of Maithili.[[3]](#cite_note-Driem2022-3)
* Several other dialects of Maithili are spoken in India and Nepal, including Dehati, Deshi, Kisan, Bantar, Barmeli, Musar, Tati and Jolaha. All the dialects are intelligible to native Maithili speakers.[[12]](#cite_note-e27-12)

## Origin and history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maithili_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Origin and history")]

The name Maithili is derived from the word [Mithila](/wiki/Mithila_(ancient) "Mithila (ancient)"), an ancient kingdom of which King [Janaka](/wiki/Janaka "Janaka") was the ruler (see [Ramayana](/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana")). Maithili is also one of the names of [Sita](/wiki/Sita "Sita"), the wife of King [Rama](/wiki/Rama "Rama") and daughter of King [Janaka](/wiki/Janaka "Janaka").[[36]](#cite_note-Pauwels2007-36)

The beginning of Maithili language and literature can be traced back to the '[Charyapadas](/wiki/Charyapada "Charyapada")', a form of Buddhist mystical verses, composed during the period of 700-1300 AD. These padas were written in [Sandhya bhasa](/wiki/Twilight_language "Twilight language") by several Siddhas who belonged to [Vajrayana Buddhism](/wiki/Vajrayana_Buddhism "Vajrayana Buddhism") and were scattered throughout the territory of [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam"), [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"), [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") and [Odisha](/wiki/Odisha "Odisha"). Several of the Siddhas were from the Mithila region such as Kanhapa, Sarhapa etc. Scholars such as [Rahul Sankrityanan](/wiki/Rahul_Sankrityayan "Rahul Sankrityayan"), Subhadra Jha and [Jayakant Mishra](/wiki/Jayakant_Mishra "Jayakant Mishra") provided evidence and proved that the language of [Charyapada](/wiki/Charyapada "Charyapada") has traces of ancient Maithili or proto Maithili.[[37]](#cite_note-37)
Apart from Charyapadas, there has been a rich tradition of folk culture, folk songs and which were popular among the common folks of the Mithila region.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

After the fall of the [Pala Empire](/wiki/Pala_Empire "Pala Empire"), disappearance of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), establishment of the [Karnat dynasty](/wiki/Karnat_dynasty "Karnat dynasty") and patronage of Maithili under Harisimhadeva (1226–1324) of Karnāta dynasty dates back to the 14th century (around 1327 AD). [Jyotirishwar Thakur](/wiki/Jyotirishwar_Thakur "Jyotirishwar Thakur") (1280–1340) wrote a unique work *[Varna Ratnakara](/wiki/Varna_Ratnakara "Varna Ratnakara")* in Maithili prose.[[39]](#cite_note-39) The *Varṇa Ratnākara* is the earliest known prose text, written by [Jyotirishwar Thakur](/wiki/Jyotirishwar_Thakur "Jyotirishwar Thakur") in [Mithilakshar](/wiki/Mithilakshar "Mithilakshar") script,[[24]](#cite_note-Yadav1979-24) and is the first prose work not only in Maithili but in any modern Indian language.[[40]](#cite_note-Maithili_literature-40)

In 1324, [Ghyasuddin Tughluq](/wiki/Ghiyath_al-Din_Tughluq "Ghiyath al-Din Tughluq"), the emperor of [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi") invaded Mithila, defeated [Harisimhadeva](/wiki/Harisimhadeva "Harisimhadeva"), entrusted Mithila to his family priest and a great Military Scholar Kameshvar Jha, a [Maithil](/wiki/Maithil "Maithil") [Brahmin](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin") of the [Oiniwar Dynasty](/wiki/Oiniwar_Dynasty "Oiniwar Dynasty"). But the disturbed era did not produce any literature in Maithili until [Vidyapati](/wiki/Vidyapati "Vidyapati") Thakur (1360 to 1450), who was an epoch-making poet under the patronage of [Shiva Simha Singh](/wiki/Shiva_Simha_Singh "Shiva Simha Singh") and his queen [Lakhimadevi](/wiki/Lakhimadevi "Lakhimadevi"). He produced over 1,000 immortal songs in Maithili on the theme of love of [Radha](/wiki/Radha "Radha") and [Krishna](/wiki/Krishna "Krishna") and the domestic life of [Shiva](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva") and [Parvati](/wiki/Parvati "Parvati") as well as on the subject of suffering of migrant labourers of [Morang](/wiki/Morang_District "Morang District") and their families; besides, he wrote a number of treaties in [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"). His [love-songs](/wiki/Love_song "Love song") spread far and wide in no time and enchanted [saints](/wiki/Saint "Saint"), [poets](/wiki/Poet "Poet") and [youth](/wiki/Youth "Youth"). [Chaitanya Mahaprabhu](/wiki/Chaitanya_Mahaprabhu "Chaitanya Mahaprabhu") saw the divine light of love behind these songs, and soon these songs became themes of [Vaishnavism](/wiki/Vaishnavism "Vaishnavism") in [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"). As a young man, [Rabindranath Tagore](/wiki/Rabindranath_Tagore "Rabindranath Tagore"), driven by curiosity and a spirit of experimentation, composed a series of poems in imitation of these songs under the pseudonym [Bhanusimha](/wiki/Bhanusimha_Thakurer_Padabali "Bhanusimha Thakurer Padabali"). Vidyapati influenced the religious literature of [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam"), [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"), and [Utkala Kingdom](/wiki/Utkala_Kingdom "Utkala Kingdom"). The blending of languages during the later period gave rise to artificial literary dialects like [Brajabuli](/wiki/Brajabuli "Brajabuli") in Bengal, and [Brajavali](/wiki/Brajavali_dialect "Brajavali dialect") in Assam.[[41]](#cite_note-Majumdar1960-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Banglapedia-Brajabuli-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

The earliest reference to Maithili or Tirhutiya is in Amaduzzi's preface to Beligatti's *Alphabetum Brammhanicum*, published in 1771.[[44]](#cite_note-Tourutiana-44) This contains a list of Indian languages among which is 'Tourutiana.' Colebrooke's essay on the Sanskrit and Prakrit languages, written in 1801, was the first to describe Maithili as a distinct dialect.[[45]](#cite_note-Colebrooke's-45)

Many devotional songs were written by Vaisnava saints, including in the mid-17th century, Vidyapati and Govindadas. Mapati Upadhyaya wrote a drama titled *Pārijātaharaṇa* in Maithili. Professional troupes, mostly from [dalit](/wiki/Dalit "Dalit") classes known as [Kirtanias](/wiki/Kirtan "Kirtan"), the singers of [bhajan](/wiki/Bhajan "Bhajan") or devotional songs, started to perform this drama in public gatherings and the courts of the nobles.
Lochana (c. 1575 – c. 1660) wrote *Rāgatarangni*, a significant treatise on the science of music, describing the rāgas, tālas, and lyrics prevalent in Mithila.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

During the [Malla dynasty](/wiki/Malla_(Nepal) "Malla (Nepal)")'s rule Maithili spread far and wide throughout [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") from the 16th to the 17th century.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) During this period, at least seventy Maithili dramas were produced. In the drama *Harishchandranrityam* by Siddhinarayanadeva (1620–57), some characters speak pure colloquial Maithili, while others speak [Bengali language](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), Sanskrit or [Prakrit](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit").[[49]](#cite_note-49) One notable Malla King who patronised Maithili was [Bhupatindra Malla](/wiki/Bhupatindra_Malla "Bhupatindra Malla") who composed 26 plays in the Maithili language during his lifetime.[[50]](#cite_note-Yadav2011-50)

After the demise of Maheshwar Singh, the ruler of [Darbhanga Raj](/wiki/Raj_Darbhanga "Raj Darbhanga"), in 1860, the Raj was taken over by the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") as regent. The Darbhanga Raj returned to his successor, Maharaj [Lakshmishvar Singh](/wiki/Lakshmeshwar_Singh "Lakshmeshwar Singh"), in 1898. The Zamindari Raj had a lackadaisical approach toward Maithili. The use of Maithili language was revived through personal efforts of MM Parameshvar Mishra, Chanda Jha, Munshi Raghunandan Das and others.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Publication of *Maithil Hita Sadhana* (1905), *Mithila Moda* (1906), and *Mithila Mihir* (1908) further encouraged writers. The first social organisation, Maithil Mahasabha,[[53]](#cite_note-53) was established in 1910 for the development of Mithila and Maithili. It blocked its membership for people outside of the [Maithil Brahmin](/wiki/Maithil_Brahmin "Maithil Brahmin") and [Karna Kayastha](/wiki/Maithil_Karna_Kayastha "Maithil Karna Kayastha") castes. Maithil Mahasabha campaigned for the official recognition of Maithili as a regional language. [Calcutta University](/wiki/Calcutta_University "Calcutta University") recognised Maithili in 1917, and other universities followed suit.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Babu Bhola Lal Das wrote *Maithili Grammar* (*Maithili Vyakaran*). He edited a book *Gadya Kusumanjali* and edited a journal *Maithili*.[[55]](#cite_note-55)
In 1965, Maithili was officially accepted by [Sahitya Academy](/wiki/Sahitya_Academy "Sahitya Academy"), an organisation dedicated to the promotion of [Indian literature](/wiki/Indian_literature "Indian literature").[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)

In 2002, Maithili was recognised on the [VIII schedule of the Indian Constitution](/wiki/Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India "Eighth Schedule to the Constitution of India") as a major Indian language; Maithili is now one of the twenty-two [Scheduled languages of India](/wiki/Languages_of_India#Scheduled_languages "Languages of India").[[58]](#cite_note-Singh2011-58)

The publishing of Maithili books in [Mithilakshar](/wiki/Tirhuta_script "Tirhuta script") script was started by [Acharya Ramlochan Saran](/wiki/Acharya_Ramlochan_Saran "Acharya Ramlochan Saran").[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

## Demand of Classical Language status for Maithili

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maithili_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Demand of Classical Language status for Maithili")]

On 3 October 2024, the Union Government of India accorded additional five languages of India as the [classical status](/wiki/Classical_languages_of_India "Classical languages of India"). These additional five languages were Assamese, Bengali, Marathi, Pali and Prakrit. But due to the lack of proposal from the Bihar government, the Maithili language missed out the status of classical language in India.[[61]](#cite_note-61) After the Union Cabinet's approval of classical language recognition for the additional five languages in India, the campaign for classical language status for Maithili gained renewed momentum.[[62]](#cite_note-62) On 7 October 2024, JD(U) national working president cum Rajya Sabha MP [Sanjay Jha](/wiki/Sanjay_Kumar_Jha "Sanjay Kumar Jha") raised the demand for classical language status to Maithili, through his social media handle.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

#### Stops

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maithili_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Stops")]

There are four series of stops- [bilabials](/wiki/Bilabial_stop "Bilabial stop"), [coronals](/wiki/Coronal_stop "Coronal stop"), [retroflex](/wiki/Retroflex_stop "Retroflex stop") and [velar](/wiki/Velar_stop "Velar stop"), along with an [affricate](/wiki/Palatal_affricate "Palatal affricate") series. All of them show the four way contrast like most of the modern [Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages"):

* [tenuis](/wiki/Tenuis_consonant "Tenuis consonant"), as /p/, which is like ⟨p⟩ in English *spin*
* [voiced](/wiki/Voice_(phonetics) "Voice (phonetics)"), as /b/, which is like ⟨b⟩ in English *bin*
* [aspirated](/wiki/Aspirated_consonant "Aspirated consonant"), as /pʰ/, which is like ⟨p⟩ in English *pin*, and
* [murmured](/wiki/Breathy_voice "Breathy voice") or [aspirated voiced](/wiki/Aspirated_voiced "Aspirated voiced"), as /bʱ/.

Apart from the retroflex series, all the rest four series show full phonological contrast in all positions. The retroflex tenius /ʈ/ and /ʈʰ/ show full contrast in all positions. /ɖ/ and /ɖʱ/ show phonological contrast mainly word-initially.[[66]](#cite_note-:1-66) Both are defective phonemes, occurring intervocalically and word finally only if preceded by a nasal consonant. Word finally and postvocalically, /ɖʱ/ surfaces as [ɽʱ~rʱ].[[67]](#cite_note-:0-67) Non-initially, both are interchangeable with [ɽ~ɾ] and [ɽʱ~rʱ] respectively.[[66]](#cite_note-:1-66)

#### Fricatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maithili_language&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Fricatives")]

/s/ and /ɦ/ are most common fricatives. They show full phonological opposition. [ɕ], which is present in [tatsama](/wiki/Tatsama "Tatsama") words, is replaced by /s/ most of the time, when independent, and prevocalic [ʂ] is replaced by /kʰ/, [x][[66]](#cite_note-:1-66) or /s/. [ɕ] occurs before /tɕ/ and [ʂ] before /ʈ/. [x] and [f] occurs in Perso-Arabic loanwords, generally replaced by /kʰ/ and /pʰ/ respectively. [x] and [ɸ] also occurs in Sanskrit words ([jihvamuliya and upadhmaniya](/wiki/Visarga "Visarga")), which is peculiar to Maithili.

#### Sonorants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maithili_language&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Sonorants")]

/m/ and /n/ are present in all phonological positions. /ŋ/ occurs only non-initially and is followed by a homorganic stop, which may be deleted if voiced, which leads to the independent presence of /ŋ/. /ɳ/ occurs non-initially, followed by a homorganic stop, and is independent only in tatsama words, which is often replaced with /n/. [ɲ] occurs only non-initially and is followed by a homorganic stop always. It is the only nasal which does not occur independently.

* In most styles of pronunciation, the retroflex flap [ɽ] occurs marginally. It is usually pronounced as an alveolar tap [ɾ] sound, and is often interchanged with /r/.[[66]](#cite_note-:1-66)
* Approximant sounds [ʋ, w, j] and fricative sounds [ɸ, f, z, ɕ, ʑ, ʂ, x], mainly occur in words that are borrowed from Sanskrit or in words of Perso-Arabic origin. The conjunct ष्प ([IAST](/wiki/IAST "IAST") *ṣp*) is pronounced [ɸp] in Maithili e.g. पुष्प [puɸp(ə)]. The conjunct ह्य (IAST *hy*) is pronounced [ɦʑ] as in ग्रऻह्य (*grahya*) [graɦʑə].[[66]](#cite_note-:1-66)

There are four non-syllabic vowels in Maithili-
i̯, u̯, e̯, o̯ written in Devanagari as य़, व़, य़ॆ, व़ॊ. Most of the times, these are written without nukta.

### Pronouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maithili_language&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Pronouns")]

Main article: [Maithili grammar § Pronouns](/wiki/Maithili_grammar#Pronouns "Maithili grammar")

Pronouns in Maithili are declined in similar way to nominals, though in most pronouns the genitive case has a different form. The lower forms below are accusative and postpositional. The plurals are formed periphrastically.

## Orthography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maithili_language&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Orthography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/The_consonants_of_the_Mithilakshar_script_and_the_corresponding_Devnagari.jpg/250px-The_consonants_of_the_Mithilakshar_script_and_the_corresponding_Devnagari.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_consonants_of_the_Mithilakshar_script_and_the_corresponding_Devnagari.jpg)

Consonants in [Tirhuta script](/wiki/Tirhuta_script "Tirhuta script")

Beginning in the 14th century, the language was written in the [Tirhuta script](/wiki/Tirhuta_script "Tirhuta script") (also known as Mithilakshara or Maithili), which is related to the [Bengali-Assamese script](/wiki/Bengali-Assamese_script "Bengali-Assamese script").[[68]](#cite_note-Pandey2009-68) By the early 20th century, this script was largely associated with the Mithila Brahmans, with most others using [Kaithi](/wiki/Kaithi_script "Kaithi script"), and [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") spreading under the influence of the scholars at [Banaras](/wiki/Banaras "Banaras").[[69]](#cite_note-Brass1974-69) Throughout the course of the century, Devanagari grew in use eventually replacing the other two, and has since remained the dominant script for Maithili.[[70]](#cite_note-Yadava2013-70)[[68]](#cite_note-Pandey2009-68)[[69]](#cite_note-Brass1974-69) Tirhuta retained some specific uses (on signage in north Bihar as well as in religious texts, genealogical records and letters), and has seen a resurgence of interest in the 21st century.[[68]](#cite_note-Pandey2009-68)

The [Tirhuta](/wiki/Tirhuta_(Unicode_block) "Tirhuta (Unicode block)") and [Kaithi](/wiki/Kaithi_(Unicode_block) "Kaithi (Unicode block)") scripts are both currently included in Unicode.

## Sample Text

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maithili_language&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Sample Text")]

The following sample text is Maithili translation of Article 1 of the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights"):

**Maithili in the [Tirhuta](/wiki/Tirhuta "Tirhuta") alphabet**

**𑒁𑒢𑒳𑒔𑓂𑒕𑒹𑒠 𑓑:** 𑒮𑒦 𑒧𑒰𑒢𑒫 𑒖𑒢𑓂𑒧𑒞𑓁 𑒮𑓂𑒫𑒞𑒢𑓂𑒞𑓂𑒩 𑒁𑒕𑒱 𑒞𑒟𑒰 𑒑𑒩𑒱𑒧𑒰 𑒂 𑒁𑒡𑒱𑒏𑒰𑒩𑒧𑒹 𑒮𑒧𑒰𑒢 𑒁𑒕𑒱। 𑒮𑒦𑒏𑒹𑒿 𑒁𑒣𑒢–𑒁𑒣𑒢 𑒥𑒳𑒠𑓂𑒡𑒱 𑒂 𑒫𑒱𑒫𑒹𑒏 𑒕𑒻𑒏 𑒂𑒍𑒩 𑒮𑒦𑒏𑒹𑒿 𑒋𑒏 𑒠𑒼𑒮𑒩𑒏 𑒣𑓂𑒩𑒞𑒱 𑒮𑒾𑒯𑒰𑒩𑓂𑒠𑒣𑒴𑒩𑓂𑒝 𑒫𑓂𑒨𑒫𑒯𑒰𑒩 𑒏𑒩𑒥𑒰𑒏 𑒔𑒰𑒯𑒲।‎

**Maithili in the [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") alphabet**

**अनुच्छेद १:** सभ मानव जन्मतः स्वतन्त्र अछि' तथा गरिमा आ अधिकारमे समान अछि'। सभकेँ अपन–अपन बुद्धि आ विवेक छैक आऒर सभकेँ एक दॊसराक प्रति सौहार्दपूर्ण व्यवहार करबाक चाही। [[73]](#cite_note-UDHR_Maithili-82)

**Roman transliteration of Maithili**

**Anucchēda Ēka:** Sabha mānaba janmataḥ svatantra achi tathā garimā ā adhikāramē samāna achi. Sabhakē̃ apana-apana buddhi ā bibeka chaika āora sabhakē̃ eka dosarāka prati sauhārdapūrṇa byabahāra karabāka cāhī.

**Translation**

**Article 1:** All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They possess conscience and reason. Therefore, everyone should act in a spirit of brotherhood towards each other.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maithili_language&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-73)** Grierson and Hoernle used ◌॰ for pronounced schwa, and ◌• for the absent schwa in some of his works.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-74)** Sometimes ◌' or even ◌ऽ is used for word final pronounced schwa, however not all of them are treated same, often only used to avoid confusion.
3. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-supA_75-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-supA_75-1) The notation a can be used for non-final weak schwa, Maithili weakens the non-final schwa, instead of deleting it (pronounced as /ə̆/). Grierson also used a for the purpose, but sometimes used apostrophe (') also for the purpose.
4. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-final-writ_76-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-final-writ_76-1) Final schwa is deleted in Maithili, though written in native scripts.
5. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-older_texts_77-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-older_texts_77-1) In older texts, ◌꣱ or ः are used, not in current use though.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-78)** ◌' is used only word finally.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-79)** Grierson used ि् .
8. **[^](#cite_ref-80)** Grierson used ु् .
9. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-recent_81-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-recent_81-1) Occurrence of æ~ɛ is a recent phenomenon.
